macro_segments.title: Introduction to Perek HaSocher Et HaPoel: The Prohibition of Wages for Work with Yayin Nesech

micro_segments.title: Opening remarks and introduction to the fifth chapter of Avodah Zarah
The Mishnah: Hiring a worker for yayin nesech production—wages are prohibited
The Mishnah: Renting a donkey for transporting yayin nesech—wages are prohibited unless incidental

macro_segments.title: Why Are Wages Prohibited? The Principle of Tofes Damav and Comparison to Orlah and Kil'ei Hakerem

micro_segments.title: The Gemara's opening question: What is the reason wages are prohibited for yayin nesech work?
First approach: The prohibition of benefit (issur hana'ah) and comparison to orlah and kil'ei hakerem
The money of prohibited items latches onto the status—tofes damav principle explained

macro_segments.title: Shemittah Fruits and the Problem of Tofes Damav: Comparison to Yayin Nesech

micro_segments.title: Shemittah fruits cannot be sold; discussion of eating vs. selling and the status of the fruits
The Mishnah on hiring workers for shemittah vegetables with explicit money vs. without mention of wages
Debate between Rambam and Ramban on the positive commandment to eat shemittah fruits
Exporting shemittah fruits from Israel and the rules of terumah and maaser

macro_segments.title: Three Explanations for the Difference Between Yayin Nesech and Shemittah: Rabbi Yochanan, Abaye, and Rava

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Yochanan's explanation: The stringency on yayin nesech and chamarin (donkey drivers) is a special kenas (fine) imposed by the Rabbis
Abaye's explanation: The money is actually given to workers in shemittah fruits as payment, permissible because given b'tzad heter (in a permissible manner)
Rava's explanation: The money does retain holiness of shemittah; the difference is that workers' wages are not subject to the fine, only skilled workers like donkey drivers
Distinguishing the stringency on yayin nesech from shemittah practices; summary of the machlokes

macro_segments.title: The Question of Stam Yeinam (Non-Jewish Wine): Do Wages from Work with Stam Yeinam Follow Yayin Nesech Rules?

micro_segments.title: Raising the question: Would the stringency of prohibited wages apply to stam yeinam as it does to yayin nesech?
Considering whether stam yeinam has the same level of prohibition and tumah as yayin nesech

macro_segments.title: The Case of Renting a Boat for Stam Yeinam: Burning and Burying Prohibited Items

micro_segments.title: A man rents a boat for transporting stam yeinam and receives wheat as payment; he asks Rav Chisda for guidance
Rav Chisda's ruling: Burn the wheat and bury the ashes in a cemetery
Principles of disposal from Masechet Temurah: Burning vs. burying and the creation of new forms (panim chadashot)
Why both burning and burying? The concern that people will find buried wheat or use ashes as fertilizer
Comparison to tools of execution buried with executed criminals; the difference in preventing misuse

macro_segments.title: Shemittah Fruits as Collateral: The Loan Arrangement of Rabbi Yannai's Students and the Principle of Time Delay

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Yannai's students lend money against shemittah fruits, collected in the seventh year, repaid in the eighth year
Rabbi Yochanan approves the practice as proper; explaining how this avoids the prohibition of selling shemittah fruits
The principle: When payment is delayed (future transaction), the sanctity does not attach to the money as it would in an immediate exchange

macro_segments.title: Etnan Zonah (Payment for Prostitution): When Does Payment Achieve the Status of Prohibited Offering?

micro_segments.title: Introduction to etnan zonah and mechir kelev from Masechet Temurah; animals paid to prostitutes are prohibited as offerings
The Braita: Payment permissible if paid before or after the act, but not if timing is simultaneous
Clarification of the Braita: Must explain what 'payment given in advance or after' means in the context of etnan
Rabbi Elazar's solution: The animal must be acquired before or after the act of intercourse; if given in advance and already brought as an offering, it is permitted
Further refinement: The man must tell the woman to acquire the animal now or hold it as collateral until intercourse occurs, allowing conditional acquisition

topical_tags.term: Le'ochla Ve'lo Le'sechora (For eating, not for business)
Rambam vs. Ramban debate on shemittah
Panim Chadashot (New form created by burning)
Rabbi Yannai's students (loan arrangement with shemittah fruits)
Masechet Temurah (Reference to laws of offerings)
Conditional acquisition (Acquire now or hold as collateral)
